### Post by travm on 2008-09-28
Yeah, I believe I have this same modem...
The easiest way for me to get it to work was to use the network applet on the menu-bar.

Begin by clicking on the network applet.

Select - "manual configuration"

Click - Unlock, and enter your password.

Select - point to point connection

click - properties


Under the General Tab,
[LIST=1]
[*]enable the connection
[*]set connection type to serial modem
[*]enter your isp phone number, dial prefix, and your account information
[/LIST]

Switch to the Modem Tab
[LIST=1]
[*]Change modem port to read "/dev/ttyACM0"
[/LIST]

That should be enough.  
To connect simply click on the network applet,
there should be a sub-menu called Dial-up connections, with a connect option and a disconnect option.  
There is no dialog box, or any information fed back, but for me i just wait a couple minutes and then my connection is active.  

If this doesn't work you can try using some commandline options, but when I was troubleshooting my modem I couldnt get it to work on the commandline.

### Post by ModelM on 2008-09-28
> **MacDuff said:**
> travm:

2. Point to point connection - **Type [color=blue]pppoe[/color]** Ethernet Interface: eth0
The check box has nothing it in.  If I click on the box a checkmark appears but it soon disappears.
If I try to change the properties for the point to connection, under the Modem tab Modem Settings, it has Ethernet interface and in the box is eth0.
Mac

You do not want the connection type to be pppoe. You want the connection type to be "serial modem".

pppoe is "ppp over ethernet" and that expects a connection through the ethernet port, not a dialup modem.

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-29
Maybe Firefox starts in offline mode? You can switch to online mode by clicking <File> and unchecking the "Work Offline" box in firefox.

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-29
gnome-ppp is just the graphical frontend for wvdial, so theoretically if wvdial works, gnome-ppp should also work. Maybe you can compare the configuration files of wvdial and gnome-ppp to see if there are perceptible differences?

FYI, the config file for wvdial is '/etc/wvdial.conf' (without the quotes) while gnome-ppp keeps its config file hidden in your home directory (i.e. '/home/<your user id>/.wvdial.conf', take note of the '.' before the word 'wvdial').

If you can't figure out the difference, post both config files here and I'll take a look.

-------

BTW, if you're successful sorting out the gnome-ppp mess, you probably would like to set things up so that firefox always starts in on-line mode so your friend won't have to uncheck the 'Work Offline' box in firefox every time. I know how to do this but would rather not complicate things for now. Just remind me later in thie thread.

---

well, if it wont allow you to enter /dev/ttyACM0 i think i might know whats going on.  I couldnt remember if i had an issue when i first set this up or not.  
What i did originally was select /dev/modem
next I had to type the following into the command line
```
sudo ln -s /dev/ttyACM0 /dev/modem
```
The problem with this was that it must be re typed in after every reboot.
so one day i tried to type in /dev/ttyACM0 into the modem port and it just worked.  If i think back i believe i wasn't allowed to type that into the modem port either.  but after using it for awhile it allowed me to type it in.  I actually might have even been using the connection when i made the change.
On a side note, i had no luck with wvdial.  I dont know why, but it did something similar to what you are describing.  During my install i read through the user guide for the modem a few times and it mentioned for other versions of linux (other than those supported) you might have to do a "ln -s /dev/ttyACM0 /dev/modem".

As for your application.  The applet does give no verification it is working. I have to rely on the lights on my modem to tell me I am connected.  but it does work.

Hope this helps in some way.

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-29
Let's try setting carrier check to off and see if it makes a difference. This is iffy since some modems require this to be off, some don't.

You don't have to edit the config file manually. Just fire up gnome-ppp. I'm sure there's a way to set this graphically.

------

If the above doesn't work and you're game, we can try the brute-force approach. :) This means backing up the original gnome-ppp config file in case we mess up, then copy/paste the entire wvdial config file unto the gnome-ppp config file. Here are the steps:

1. back up the orig gnome-ppp config file:

```
cp /home/max/.wvdial.conf /home/max/.wvdial.conf.orig
```
2. open both config files:
```
sudo gedit /etc/wvdial.conf
gedit /home/max/.wvdial.conf
```
3. copy/paste so that the content of /home/max/.wvdial.conf looks exactly the same as /etc/wvdial.conf

BTW, you should understand that I'm doing this from memory. I haven't used wvdial nor gnome-ppp in the last 18 months.

----------

If the above still doesn't work, we can try using a different graphical dialer program called KPPP. This however will require downloading 30+ MB worth of files from the ubuntu repos. But let's cross the bridge when we get there. I'm just putting it here so you don't lose hope yet. :)

---

KPPP error
The pppd daemon died unexpectedly!
{This seems to be the same error message always reported by GNOME-PPP}

Exit status: 2
See 'man pppd' for an explanation of the error codes or take a look at the kppp FAQ on [http://developer.kde.org/~kppp/index.html](http://developer.kde.org/~kppp/index.html)

{Exit status 2 is defined as: An error was detected in processing the options given,  such  as two mutually exclusive options being used.
At one point a day or so ago, I got an Exit status 2 error on GNOME-PPP as well}

Sep 29 20:48:55 T-61 pppd[6668]: Can't open options file /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options: Permission denied

I had a look at /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options and found that Owner/Group was set to "root:30"  which is what GNOME-PPP had originally set in its Owner/Group when it was first installed. 

I changed the Group to root and when I tried running KPPP again, it connected to the ISP but when trying to load a web page, the modem data light went out just before the page loaded and the KPPP log reported a disconnect.

I retried and that time I was able to load several web pages but they seemed to load slower than when using WvDial.

Do you know of any problem with my having changed permissions on /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options?

Obviously GNOME-PPP and KPPP are having somewhat the same difficulties but KPPP reports it more thoroughly.

Does this suggest a "Eureka" to you and do you have any other comments or suggestions regarding GNOME-PPP?

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-30
I was hoping to spare you the trouble of downloading KPPP, but since you did it already, let's focus on KPPP instead of gnome-ppp. (It's actually my dialer app of choice).

Funny thing is, I've been using KPPP with ubuntu since Breezy 5.10 about 3 years ago, have kept up with all the ubuntu versions through the years, and never encountered the permissions problem.

Anyway, let's take a stab at solving the KPPP permissions problem. Enter the following command in the terminal:```
groups mac
```

This will list all the groups that your user id (mac) belongs to. We want to make sure that mac is a member of the 'dip' group. If you don't see 'dip' in the list, then we have our culprit.

Let's also double-check if the file /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options is as it should be. Use nautilus file browser and navigate to the said file. Right click on the file and choose 'Properties'. A new window will pop up. Click on the 'Permissions' tab. On a default install, the file should be owned by 'root' and the group should be 'dip'.

Let me know what happened with the 'groups mac' command. If we're lucky, it may just be a matter of adding mac to the 'dip' group.

BTW, this is unfamiliar territory to me. These info I'm passing on are things I read about when I was struggling with dial-up 3 years ago but never really had to do. As I mentioned, I didn't have to take this route at all with KPPP. I'm just guessing that your user settings may be different from mine.

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-30
Don't worry about sticky. It probably has nothing to do with the problem.

Let's just focus on one thing for now: the group permission for the file /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options. Right now it is set to 'root'. It should be set to 'dip' (without the quotes). Here's how to do it:

1. open a terminal and enter the following command:
```
gksudo nautilus
```

We are going to run the nautilus file browser as root, so you can change permissions for the file in question. Be very careful while you're in this mode. As the root user, you can change practically anything in your system. It can also mess up your system big-time.

2. Like before, navigate all the way to the file /etc/ppp/peers/kppp-options. Right-click on the file, choose 'Properties' and go to the 'Permissions' tab.

3. Unlike before, you'll notice that the drop-down box for group will be active. Use this box to change group to 'dip'.

4. close the window and exit nautilus.

5. Let's try using kppp one more time. I'm hoping this will solve the permissions problem.

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-09-30
> Ok duncan, I changed the group to dip.
The owner has read/write privileges
The group has read only privileges
No execution as program is permitted.

Is this as it should be?

Yes. The settings are OK.

> By the way GNOME-PPP still fails but I will just install KPPP on Duncan's computer. Not having used a computer ever, he will not know the difference.


My hunch is, if we apply the same permission changes to the file /etc/ppp/peers/wvdial, gnome-ppp will also work.

> I guess we can consider this problem solved or is there more information you want to impart.... like how to prevent Firefox from defaulting to work off line when connected to a dial up Internet connection.

It never happens to my own machines which are connected to a LAN but always happens when I have been testing this dial-up connection.

The fix will involve disabling the Network Manager applet during ubuntu startup. This will work in Duncan's case, since he will only be using dial-up to connect, but may not be feasible in your own machine if you need the Network Manager to connect to LAN. I'll just provide the details and let you decide to use it or not.

1. We need to create 2 files with only the word 'exit' (without quotes) in them.

```
sudo gedit /etc/default/NetworkManager
sudo gedit /etc/default/NetworkManagerDispatcher
```

2. Save the files, then reboot. You'll notice that the Network Manager applet will be disappear from the notification area.

3. Firefox and evolution will now default to on-line mode when started.

4. If for some reason you need to enable Network Manager again, you can simply delete or rename the 2 files we created, reboot, and the Network Manager applet will once again appear in the notification area. However, firefox/evolution will once again default to offline mode.


------------------------

This is a bit off-topic. I'm just curious about the output of the 'groups mac' command we had earlier. I find it odd that 'mac' is a member of the group 'root'. Did you by any chance played around with setting user privileges for 'mac'?

I'm not an expert on the subject. But I have a sneaky feeling it's the main reason causing all these problems with gnome-ppp and kppp, hence the need to reset privileges for essential files back to 'dip'. It's just a hunch though. I'm sure there are more qualified people here on this forum who can comment on this.

-------------------------

Good luck setting up ubuntu and dial-up networking for Duncan!

---

### Post by MacDuff on 2008-09-30
Many thanks duncan.  I will make notes of all your suggestions and take them with me this weekend.   

You asked about "mac" being a member of the group "root".  It is a long story, about a 16 months long, but the short version is that, knowing nothing at all about Linux I decided to try Ubuntu, saw the potential and made the switch on one of our computers, then the second one, then the third one.  All this took place much too fast because the networking turned out to be a nightmare for someone who knew as little as I did.  

As a result I was thrashing about, trying to keep all the balls in the air.  Most advice from Ubuntu users was excellent, well thought out and presented in an easy to understand way like yours is.  Other advice, though well intentioned, was not accurate or was obscure,  and some was misleading to a newbie like me. 

But I kept trying and sometimes resorted to blindly changing things in order to try to get applications working.  I am sure that at some point I must have made mac a member of root to force something to work and then forgot about it.

Should I be changing permissions back to some "standard".  If so, is there an easy way to do that?  

There have been about a dozen users whose support through the forum has been outstanding, You are one of them.  You are patient with us newbies, you present your advice clearly and completely without resorting to cryptic suggestions that are way over the head of a newbie.

People like you are a tremendous credit to the Linux movement. I salute you.

Mac

### Post by _duncan_ on 2008-10-01
Thank you for the kind words, Mac. But as the saying goes, it takes 2 to tango. You presented the problem in an organized manner, never exhibited frustration while the people trying to help were still poking around, and no rants either! To top it all, you are doing all this to help a 70-year old friend.

Let's tie up some loose ends before this turn into a mutual admiration thread! :)

> Should I be changing permissions back to some "standard". If so, is there an easy way to do that? 

I don't think I'm knowledgeable enough to answer this. My suggestion is to create a new thread, explain the situation and potential problem, and wait for the ubuntu gurus to have their say.

---------

BTW, it may be to your best interest to try and get gnome-ppp working before this weekend. If I recall correctly, the gnome-ppp package is less than 1 MB, while kppp including its dependent packages will require 30 MB of downloads for a fresh ubuntu install. No problem if you have broadband. But over at Duncan's place, a 30 MB download takes about 2 hours on dial-up.
